english: W. prob. = lan (lan), retribution, return, *de lan-kyár yin* (de lan kyar yin) that is all he has gained by it!
Thgy., prob. = lan-čágs (lan chags); or perh. the original form of la-yógs (la yogs)?

misfortune, adversity, calamity, as a supposed punishment for what has been done in a former life; every unlucky accident, that happens to a person without his own fault, being looked upon as a retribution for former crimes. Thus lan-čags (lan chags) denotes about what Non-Buddhists would call destiny, fate, disaster.

braid, plait, tress of hair (Cs. curl, lock of hair? Sch. pigtail?) lán-bu (lan bu) slé-ba (sle ba) or lhé-ba (lhe ba) to make plaits, to plait the hair; lan-tsár (lan tsar) ornaments, worn in the hair Mil.

, more accurately lantsha (lan tsha) (acc. to Hodgson corrupted from रञ्जा) n. of a style of writing in use among Nepalese Buddhists. It is a kind of ornamental writing, used by caligraphists for inscriptions and titles of books.

salt, prob. = tswa (tshwa), lán-tswa (lan tshwa) čú-la (chu la) tim-pa (thim pa) salt which dissolves in water Thgy.; lán-tswa (lan tshwa) ḱa-zás (kha zas) kún-gyi (kun gyi) bró-ba (bro ba) skyed (skyed) salt gives a relish to every dish S.g.; lán-tswai (lan tshwa'i) ču (chu) salt-water Lex.; lán-tswa-ba (lan tshwa ba) saline, briny Med.

to speak, talk, tell, mi-la (mi la) ma (ma) lab (lab) do not tell anybody Mil.; rdzún-ytam (rdzun gtam) láb-pa (lab pa) Bhot. to lie, to utter a falsehood; lab tsól-ba (lab tsol ba) Sch.: 'to speak unseemly, to brawl(?)'. -- lab tsám-pa (lab tsam pa) Sch.: to speak while dreaming, to be delirious. lab ytón-ba (lab gton ba) Cs., *lab gyáb-če* (lab rgyab ce) W. to talk, to chat; *ḱa-lab-den* (kha lab ldan) eloquent, fluent of words C., W.; rgya-láb (rgya lab) a great deal of talk, rgya-láb-čan (rgya lab can) talkative C., W. Comp. láb-ga (lab ga) Cs., *láb-ča* (lab cha) C., W. talk. -- lab-grógs (lab grogs) Mil. companion, intimate friend Mil. -- lab-rdöl (lab rdol) talking unbecomingly Sch. -- *láb-ra* (lab ra) (prop. láb-sgra (lab sgra)) 'noise of tattling', tattle, talk, *láb-ra tán-če* (lab ra gtong ce) W to chat, babble. -- *lab-lób* (lab lob) or lub-lo* (lub lo), with *gyáb-če* (rgyab ce) to speak indistinctly, to mumble; to speak in one's sleep; *lab-lób-te (lab lob te) dul* ('dul) he walks speaking in his sleep, he is a somnambulist W.
a heap of stones in which a pole with little flags is fastened, esp. on mountain passes Schl. 198.

1. way, road lam-čén (lam chen), rgyá-lam (rgya lam), stón-lam (stong lam) Cs., *má-lam* (ma lam) W. highway, main road, high-road; gyén-lam (gyen lam) an up-hill road, an ascent, túr-lam (thur lam) a down-hill road, préd-lam (phred lam), rtsibs-lam (rtsibs lam) a horizontal or a sloping road, that leads alongside a hill, lam-jrán (lam 'phrang) a narrow footpath, lam dóg-mo (lam dog mo) a strait path, lam yáñspa (lam yangs pa) a broad one; *lam dé-mo* (lam bde mo) a good, easy road, *lam sóg-po* (lam sog po) a difficult, dangerous, road W.; lam t́ár (lam thar) the road is open, may be passed, is not obstructed by snow etc. Glr.; lam byéd-pa (lam byed pa) Sch., *lam có-če* (lam bzo ce), *sál-če* (bsal ce) W. to clear a path, to construct a road; rgya-gár-gyi (rgya gar gyi) lam (lam) or rgya-gar-du (rgya gar du) gró-bai ('gro ba'i) lam (lam) the way to India Pth.; grí-lam (gri lam) the way of the knife, i. e. a cut, slit, slash; *j́i-ne ('di nas) dúd-pe (dud pa'i) lam* (lam) here is the way for the smoke, here the smoke escapes W. -- 2. way, space or distance travelled over, journey, lám-du (lam du) on the road, on the journey; bal-bód-kyi (bal bod kyi) lam (lam) the journey from Nepal to Tibet Glr., lám-du (lam du) júg-pa ('jug pa) to set out, to travel, also: to continue one's journey, lam-p̌yéd (lam phyed) tsám-du (tsam du) pyín-pa (phyin pa) dañ (dang) as we had done about half the way Dzl., lám-nas (lam nas) ldóg-pa (ldog pa) to return home from a journey, khrús-la (khrus la) gró-bai ('gro ba'i) lám-du (lam du) when he went to bathe Dzl. -- 3. गति, fig. way or manner of acting, in order to obtain a certain end; t́ár(-pai) (thar pa'i) lam (lam) the way of deliverance, viz. for Buddhists: from the cycle of transmigrations, for Christians: from sin and its consequences; hence the way to happiness, to eternal bliss. The six (sometimes only five) classes of beings (v. gró-ba ('gro ba)) are sometimes called the six ways of re-birth within the orb of transmigration. In mystical writings lam lńa (lam lnga) are spoken of as the ways leading to the sa bču (sa bcu) (q. v.) Thgy.; lam(-gyi) (lam gyi) rim(-pa) (rim pa) Cs.: 'a degree of advance; the several steps towards perfection'; also the title of sundry mystical writ-
